findings.summary: Glycyrrhetinic acid from licorice root inhibits CYP1A2 in hepatic microsomal assays with an IC50 consistent with exposures achieved at moderate supplemental doses; ciprofloxacin is partially cleared via CYP1A2-mediated oxidation, suggesting increased fluoroquinolone exposure with chronic licorice use.

Licorice-induced pseudoaldosteronism causes hypokalemia, which independently potentiates QT prolongation; fluoroquinolone antibiotics including ciprofloxacin carry a known QT-prolonging risk, and electrolyte imbalance synergistically amplifies this cardiac adverse effect.
findings.citation: Omar et al., CMAJ 2012; EMA fluoroquinolone cardiac safety review 2019

findings.summary: At culinary licorice exposures (confectionery), the CYP1A2 inhibitory effect is sub-threshold and the QT risk is minimal; the interaction becomes clinically relevant only with habitual medicinal licorice use (>100 mg glycyrrhizinic acid per day) sustained for more than 2 weeks.
